japanese.difficulty: Hard (for English speakers) — different writing systems, grammar structure, and honorifics

study_approach.beginner: Start with Hiragana and Katakana (2-4 weeks). Then learn the first 100-200 kanji alongside basic vocabulary and grammar. Focus on the most common 800 words for JLPT N5.
study_approach.intermediate: Build kanji knowledge to 500-1000. Focus on grammar patterns and reading practice. JLPT N3 requires ~3,000-4,000 vocabulary words.
study_approach.advanced: Immersion + targeted study of weak areas. JLPT N1 requires ~10,000 vocabulary words and ~2,000 kanji.

japanese.spatial_memory_tip: Place kanji on the grid grouped by radical or meaning. Related kanji near each other helps you see patterns (e.g., all water-related kanji in one area, all person-related kanji in another). This is especially powerful for remembering similar-looking kanji.
recommended_resources: Gridually Japanese decks — N5 through N1 vocabulary and kanji
WaniKani (kanji-specific, but Gridually's spatial approach covers this too)

spanish.difficulty: Easy-Medium (for English speakers) — similar alphabet, many cognates

study_approach.beginner: Learn the 500 most common words + present tense conjugations. Spanish has many English cognates — leverage them.
study_approach.intermediate: Expand to 2000-3000 words, past tenses, subjunctive mood. Start reading simple articles and watching shows with Spanish subtitles.
study_approach.advanced: Focus on subjunctive nuances, regional vocabulary, and idiomatic expressions. Immersion is key at this stage.

spanish.spatial_memory_tip: Group vocabulary by theme on the grid: food words together, travel words together, work vocabulary together. Place verb conjugations in a consistent pattern so the spatial position reinforces the grammar pattern.
recommended_resources: Gridually Spanish decks — beginner through advanced vocabulary

german.difficulty: Medium (for English speakers) — shared Germanic roots, but complex grammar

study_approach.beginner: Learn articles with every noun from day one (der/die/das). Focus on the 1000 most common words. German word order is different — practice with full sentences.
study_approach.intermediate: Master case system (nominative, accusative, dative, genitive). Expand vocabulary to 3000+ words. Start reading simple news articles.
study_approach.advanced: Focus on compound words, academic vocabulary, and formal/informal register. German compound words can be very long — break them down into components.

german.spatial_memory_tip: Color-code or group cards by grammatical gender (der/die/das) on the grid. This spatial grouping helps your brain associate gender with noun clusters rather than memorizing each one individually.
recommended_resources: Gridually German decks — beginner through advanced

french.difficulty: Easy-Medium (for English speakers) — many shared words with English

study_approach.beginner: Leverage English-French cognates (thousands of shared words). Focus on pronunciation early — French pronunciation is tricky but rule-based.
study_approach.intermediate: Master verb tenses (especially passé composé vs imparfait). Expand to 2500+ words.
study_approach.advanced: Focus on subjunctive, literary tenses, and cultural nuances.

french.spatial_memory_tip: Group verbs by conjugation pattern on the grid. French has many irregular verbs — cluster them spatially so you can see patterns in the irregularities.

korean.difficulty: Medium-Hard (for English speakers) — Hangul is logical, but grammar is very different

study_approach.beginner: Learn Hangul first (1-2 weeks — it's designed to be logical). Then focus on basic sentence patterns and the 500 most common words.
study_approach.intermediate: Master honorific levels (crucial in Korean). Expand vocabulary and grammar patterns. TOPIK II requires solid reading comprehension.
study_approach.advanced: Focus on nuance, hanja (Chinese characters used in Korean), and formal/academic Korean.

korean.spatial_memory_tip: Place Hangul consonants and vowels on the grid in their systematic order — Korean's alphabet was designed with a logical structure that maps well to spatial organization.

chinese.difficulty: Hard (for English speakers) — tonal language with character-based writing

study_approach.beginner: Master pinyin and the four tones first. Learn the 200 most common characters alongside basic vocabulary. HSK 1 requires ~150 words.
study_approach.intermediate: Build character knowledge to 1000+. Focus on reading and character components (radicals). HSK 4 requires ~1200 words.
study_approach.advanced: Focus on chengyu (idioms), formal writing, and reading comprehension. HSK 6 requires 5000+ words.

chinese.spatial_memory_tip: Group characters by radical on the grid. Chinese characters share components — placing characters with the same radical near each other reveals patterns and makes learning new characters faster.

general_tips: Consistency beats intensity: 15 minutes daily > 2 hours once a week
Learn vocabulary in context (sentences) not in isolation (word lists)
Use the spatial grid to organize by theme — your brain retrieves themed clusters faster
Import content from your textbook or course using Gridually's AI card generator
Set a specific, measurable goal (e.g., 'JLPT N5 by December' not 'learn Japanese')
Combine flashcard study with immersion (shows, music, podcasts in target language)
